summary: Gruber argues that Apple's M1 chip has shattered longstanding assumptions about computing trade-offs — that performance necessarily comes at the cost of heat and battery life. He contends the M1 embarrasses the entire PC industry, including Intel and AMD, and that Rosetta 2 translation is so good it defies expectations about architecture emulation. He dismantles Patrick Moorhead's skeptical Forbes review as grossly misleading, documenting Moorhead's history of underestimating Apple's silicon advances going back to the A7 chip in 2013. Gruber acknowledges real software compatibility issues exist but insists they are temporary and unrelated to the M1 hardware itself. He frames the M1 as a breakthrough comparable to the original iPhone, and argues that skeptics are clinging to outdated assumptions rather than confronting reality.
key_points: The M1 breaks the fundamental trade-off between performance, heat, and battery life that defined PC computing for decades
Rosetta 2 runs Intel Mac software as fast or faster than most actual Intel Macs, defying expectations about architecture emulation
Patrick Moorhead's skeptical review conflates real but temporary software compatibility issues with purported hardware 'warts' in the M1 itself
Moorhead has a documented track record of underestimating Apple silicon — dismissing the 64-bit A7, the iPad Pro's A9X benchmarks, and predicting Intel would get the iPad Pro contract
The M1 represents a paradigm shift comparable to the iPhone's 2007 introduction, where one company leapfrogs an entire established industry
money_quotes: Those machines are just standing around in their underwear now because the M1 stole all their pants.
M1 Macs completely upend what we can and should expect from PCs. It's a breakthrough along the lines of the iPhone itself in 2007.
The adage is, 'If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.' Emphasis on probably — the M1 Macs are an exception.
The reality of the M1 has an Apple bias. There is no 'Well, here's the downside' with regard to the state of Apple Silicon versus the entire rest of the industry.
He either should know better, or does know better and wrote this grossly misleading tripe anyway, knowing there was an Apple-skeptical audience willing to lap it up.
Soon though, that will seem utterly normal, and it'll be every other laptop in the world, the ones that do get hot and do make noise that will seem wrong and weird.
Sometimes an assertion of truthiness is actually true.
key_insight: The M1 chip didn't just improve on existing PC trade-offs — it eliminated them entirely, and the only people denying this are analysts with a track record of underestimating Apple's silicon ambitions.
